one_liner: The ketchup stain becomes an insurance claim.
search_doc:
The ketchup stain becomes an insurance claim.
Mechanisms: real-product-pseudoproduct, ivr-as-content, category-mashup-insurance, everyday-accident-monetisation, radio-hotline-mechanic
Idea: Claimants either visited heinzketchupinsurance.com or called our 24/7 ketchup insurance hotline, where they had to navigate a witty interactive menu to reach a real ketchup insurance agent. All they had to do was provide 30 seconds to identify and file their claim, creating one of the longest, most interactive ketchup-related conversations in radio history.
Insight: While we can't stop ketchup happening, we can cover them. Because if it's worth the risk, it has to be Heinz. So, inspired by 17 cost incidents listed on social media, we launched a real, legally-binding insurance program offering customers coverage against 57 ketchup catastrophes.

extracted.tagline: Turning customer accidents into real insurance claims. Turning spills into smiles.
extracted.context: We've all been there. The spills, the splats, and the splashes on your white shirt, rug, or whatever else is within pouring proximity. Everyone knows that a ketchup catastrophe is one of the most unforgiving customer pain points for Heinz. In fact, a staggering 49% of Heinz customers have had ketchup accidents.
extracted.insight: While we can't stop ketchup happening, we can cover them. Because if it's worth the risk, it has to be Heinz. So, inspired by 17 cost incidents listed on social media, we launched a real, legally-binding insurance program offering customers coverage against 57 ketchup catastrophes.
extracted.idea: Claimants either visited heinzketchupinsurance.com or called our 24/7 ketchup insurance hotline, where they had to navigate a witty interactive menu to reach a real ketchup insurance agent. All they had to do was provide 30 seconds to identify and file their claim, creating one of the longest, most interactive ketchup-related conversations in radio history.
execution: Kraft Heinz legal team created an official claim application form hosted on a dedicated claims website
30-second radio claims hotline running across UAE national radio (104.4 SUITLY) where customers could file a claim
Play-by-roleplay 104.4M ad-hosted talkshow Radio Twitter, podcast and gameshow
60-second radio ad spots running 24/7 on the radio market for 6 months, hosted on Spotify, online radio, AM/FM stations across the UAE
Approved Heinz Ighted customer recovery strategy by compensating customers with complimentary services
Through MyDhandfu - the Middle East's leading insights app

inferred.one_liner: The ketchup stain becomes an insurance claim.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a 49% customer-stain pain point into a fully operational ketchup-insurance product with an IVR hotline; generated 21M impressions, 3,056 applications and $23M in earned media.

visual.style_description: Bold red Heinz-branded case board with a hero still-life of a vintage red rotary phone, a printed Ketchup Insurance claim form and a Heinz bottle; press logos top-right, four large white metric blocks across the bottom, plus a furry-pet 'spills into smiles' photo strip.

idea.rationale: Building a legally-binding ketchup-insurance product around the everyday stain pain point is a witty category-mashup that gives the brand a new conversational territory. The audio-native expression — a 24/7 hotline with an interactive voice-menu radio claims line — is a real radio mechanic, but the idea itself originates as a product-PR construct that happens to live partly on radio rather than a purebred audio idea.

strategy.rationale: Heinz is Kraft Heinz's flagship condiment brand whose iconic ketchup bottle anchors the global tomato-ketchup category, and self-deprecating play on its own staining is on-brand humour territory the brand has explored before. The cited '49% of Heinz customers have had ketchup accidents' is the campaign's load-bearing insight but is presented as a brand-reported figure without a source on the board, and the '57 ketchup catastrophes' covered is an entertaining count rather than a verified consumer-research dataset.

execution.rationale: The radio craft is the IVR claim-line itself — a 30-second hotline routing callers through a witty interactive menu to a real agent — alongside 60-second spots across UAE AM/FM, Spotify and online radio for six months. The orchestration also includes a legal claims website and PR loop, but the board's execution copy is partly garbled and the audio-craft choices (voice direction, sound design of the menu, music) are not detailed, so the audio-craft layer that an Audio & Radio jury rewards is undocumented.

impact.rationale: '$23M in earned media' is treated as entrant-reported and unaudited; '21M impressions' is a surrogate reach metric and '856% increase in monthly engagement' is unanchored — 856% of what monthly baseline is not disclosed. '3,056 total applications in 1 month' is the closest thing to a hard behavioural outcome and is concretely anchored, but for a CPG brand the absent business outcome is ketchup volume or share lift in the UAE market. Press support across Gulf News, Adweek, Yahoo, Esquire and Sky News is a credible directional signal of regional pickup.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the ketchup-insurance construct is a charming category mashup that generated a real 3,056-application behavioural outcome, but the load-bearing 49% stat and 856% engagement lift are unsourced and unanchored, and the audio craft itself is lightly demonstrated on the board.

one_liner: The MRI's noise becomes the storybook's soundtrack.
search_doc:
The MRI's noise becomes the storybook's soundtrack.
Mechanisms: medical-noise-as-narrative, audiobook-as-anxiety-relief, sound-design-sync-to-procedure, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Siemens, a top MRI manufacturer, partnered with children's book authors to turn MRI sounds into a wondrous experience. Each Magnetic Story is customised to the sounds of the most common pediatric MRI scans, turning their dreadful sounds into a delightful narrative.
Insight: Children freeze inside MRI scanners because the rhythmic banging is terrifying; if that same rhythm became a familiar story beat, the noise stops being scary.

extracted.tagline: Turning scary MRI noises into beautiful children's audiobooks.
extracted.context: MRI sounds can be louder than a military jet. They are scary for children and they present a hurdle to care for the radiology team.
extracted.idea: Siemens, a top MRI manufacturer, partnered with children's book authors to turn MRI sounds into a wondrous experience. Each Magnetic Story is customised to the sounds of the most common pediatric MRI scans, turning their dreadful sounds into a delightful narrative.
execution: Each author wrote a Magnetic Story corresponding to the sounds of one specific MRI scan
The audiobooks play in sync with the scans, with the recitation matching up with the sound sequences in perfect timing
Library of pediatric scan-matched audiobooks (Cranial Scan, The Click Trick, Lumbar Scan, Scanner on Loan, Spine Scan, Shoulder Scan, Brain Scan, Sit on Sleep, Brain Sweet Brain, etc.)

inferred.insight: Children freeze inside MRI scanners because the rhythmic banging is terrifying; if that same rhythm became a familiar story beat, the noise stops being scary.
inferred.one_liner: The MRI's noise becomes the storybook's soundtrack.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Bespoke children's audiobooks scored to the exact rhythm of each pediatric MRI sequence reframe the scanner's banging as a story beat, easing patient anxiety and helping radiology teams perform cleaner scans.

visual.style_description: A young patient lying inside an MRI machine wearing oversized headphones, with stylised pink and blue lightning-wave graphics representing magnetic story sound emanating from the scanner; below is a strip of audiobook 'cover' thumbnails matching different scans.

review_reasons: No on-board metrics or press coverage; impact relies on qualitative summary only.

idea.rationale: Composing children's audiobooks whose narration is timed to the exact rhythmic noise pattern of each specific MRI sequence reframes the scanner's frightening banging as a story beat, turning a clinical sound problem into purposeful sound design. The idea is purely audio-native — it cannot be communicated visually because the whole device is the tempo-locked relationship between the machine's noise and the narrator's voice.

strategy.rationale: Siemens Healthineers is the medical-tech spin-off of Siemens AG and one of the world's largest MRI manufacturers, so improving pediatric scan experience is direct product-utility territory rather than borrowed-purpose advocacy. The context that MRI noise can exceed jet-engine levels is a well-documented clinical fact, and the audio-anxiety insight (sound-driven distress causing motion-corrupted scans and rescans) is observable in real radiology workflows, though the board cites no clinical data to anchor the patient-anxiety claim.

execution.rationale: Each audiobook is scored and timed to the specific acoustic signature of a named pediatric scan protocol (Cranial, Lumbar, Spine, Shoulder, Brain) with narration cued to the machine's percussive sequence, so the MRI noise becomes a deliberate rhythm element rather than ambient threat. The library spans multiple scan types with bespoke titles and authors, but the board does not show distribution into hospital networks or scanner-integration detail, leaving the deployment scale of the audio craft undocumented.

impact.rationale: The board contains no metrics and no press coverage — there is no claimed patient-anxiety reduction, no rescan-rate improvement, no scan-duration delta, no hospital uptake number. For a purpose-led product extension whose stated business outcome is cleaner scans and reduced sedation, the absence of any clinical or operational outcome on the board is the dominant Impact weakness, and the review_flag on the extraction confirms the case is qualitative only.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the rhythm-locked audiobook is genuinely audio-native craft on the right brand mission, but the case board carries no metrics or press, so the Impact case rests entirely on the elegance of the idea.

one_liner: The recruiting ad becomes a love song.
search_doc:
The recruiting ad becomes a love song.
Mechanisms: music-as-recruiting-tool, tagline-as-song, earned-pop-track, purpose-driven-employer-brand
Idea: Nationally, with food always prioritised, dishes delivered as a message to Generation Z through music. Cause to live a smile, with you 'Smile' [...] which carries the new core formats — a song which carries the new core notice. It was a beautiful new collaboration with [...] generation Z. After Gen Z hearing the song, who wanted to be the most generous brand.
Insight: Japan's shrinking Gen Z workforce no longer responds to traditional recruiting ads — but they will share a song that speaks their language.

extracted.tagline: The most loved recruiting AD. 'This one song transformed McDonald's into The Most Generous Brand.' — Contagious
extracted.context: 1.4M shops nationwide. 56 million visits every month. Behind the megaframes our Gen Z, which assumed by about 40% of the team workforce. However, with Japan-X falling, the number of crew shortages is now a serious decreasing year by year, and the company suffered from a chronic shortage of staff. McDonald's bars are well-suited to be both attractive to recruit […].
extracted.idea: Nationally, with food always prioritised, dishes delivered as a message to Generation Z through music. Cause to live a smile, with you 'Smile' [...] which carries the new core formats — a song which carries the new core notice. It was a beautiful new collaboration with [...] generation Z. After Gen Z hearing the song, who wanted to be the most generous brand.
execution: An original song built around the McDonald's 'Smile' tagline
Music video starring a young female crew member in McDonald's uniform
Distribution through radio, streaming and social
Recruiting campaign hooked to the song's release
press_coverage: Contagious — 'This one song transformed McDonald's into The Most Generous Brand.'

inferred.insight: Japan's shrinking Gen Z workforce no longer responds to traditional recruiting ads — but they will share a song that speaks their language.
inferred.one_liner: The recruiting ad becomes a love song.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a recruiting campaign into a culturally-loved pop song built around McDonald's 'Smile' tagline; drove 36M views, 100,000+ new hires and a 130% brand-love lift among Gen Z.

visual.style_description: Hero photograph of a young Japanese crew member in retro McDonald's red-and-yellow uniform holding a tray of fries and a Coca-Cola, set against a soft-focus McDonald's interior; the bold yellow 'No Smiles' wordmark sits over the image, with structured 'Issue / Idea / Result' text blocks and a bright KPI row (36M / 115% / 100,000+ / 130%) on the right, and a five-pane footer strip (The Culture / The Social / The Music / The Influencer / The Reaction).

idea.rationale: Replacing a recruiting ad with an original pop song built around the McDonald's 'Smile' tagline is a recognisable music-as-marketing approach, executed against a real employer-brand problem rather than a category-redefining audio idea. The native-to-category fit is medium — the song is genuinely a piece of music that lives on radio and streaming, but the board's case mostly leans on the music-video and social distribution layers.

strategy.rationale: McDonald's Japan is the local arm of the world's largest QSR chain and Japan's largest restaurant operator by outlet count, with Gen Z reportedly forming roughly 40% of its crew workforce against a documented backdrop of Japan's shrinking working-age population. Recruiting via a culturally-loved song fits the brand's long-standing 'i'm lovin' it' emotional-employer territory, and the demographic context (working-age decline) is a verifiable national statistic. The board's text is partly garbled, leaving the precise insight chain less crisp than it could be.

execution.rationale: The audio asset is an original Japanese pop track built around the brand's 'Smile' menu tagline, performed by a young female crew member figure and rolled out across radio, streaming, social and a recruiting funnel hook. The orchestration spans music release, music video, influencer reaction and recruiting application flow, but the board does not detail the music-production choices (composer, genre conventions, mix style) that would let an Audio & Radio jury credit the song itself as audio craft.

impact.rationale: '100,000+ new hires' and '115% of applicants vs target' are anchored business outcomes for the brand's actual problem (crew shortage), with target named even if its absolute size is not disclosed. '36M views in 3 months' is a credible reach figure for Japan but treated as entrant-reported, and '130% brand love among Gen Z' is an unanchored ratio with no defined index or comparison basis. Press support is a single Contagious quote, so the strongest part of the case is the hiring outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the song delivered a hard hiring outcome (100,000+ new crew) against a real labour-shortage problem, but the audio-craft case is lightly documented and the supporting brand-love metric is unbased.

search_doc:
The pub becomes the museum.
Mechanisms: augmented-reality-overlay, legislative-loophole-exploit, cultural-heritage-reframing, venue-as-medium, audio-guide-as-content
Idea: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and other financial benefits to places of historical significance that are open to the public, such as museums. So, Heineken turned Irish historical pubs into actual museums — making them eligible for tax reductions and allowing their rich past to protect their future.
Insight: If pubs are legally recognised as cultural heritage museums, the state will subsidise them and protect them from closure.

extracted.tagline: Turning historical pubs into real museums. Taking advantage of the governmental protection process of pieces of cultural heritage here.
extracted.context: Irish pubs have been living in a challenging economic environment; since 2005, a quarter of Irish pubs have shut. Buildings that have served the purpose and their communities for hundreds of years are at risk and cultural landmarks and the heritage they represent should be preserved as such.
extracted.idea: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and other financial benefits to places of historical significance that are open to the public, such as museums. So, Heineken turned Irish historical pubs into actual museums — making them eligible for tax reductions and allowing their rich past to protect their future.
execution: Pubs across Ireland turned into virtual museums
Through augmented reality, objects in the pubs become interactive 3D artefacts complete with descriptions, audio guides and even souvenirs
This allows pubs to begin the process of becoming accredited museums, protecting them for the years to come
Scan code, activate AR, visit the Pub Museum, buy beers and souvenirs

inferred.insight: If pubs are legally recognised as cultural heritage museums, the state will subsidise them and protect them from closure.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Irish pubs as protected cultural-heritage museums, unlocking a 20% tax cut for participating venues and broad international press pickup across heritage, marketing and business outlets.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial case board hero-shot on a moody pub interior with framed paintings; an iPhone in the centre shows the Pub Museums AR app overlay; press logo wall along the top, four small pub-front thumbnails along the bottom, callout tiles for benefits on the right.

idea.rationale: Reframing historic Irish pubs as legally accredited museums to unlock heritage tax protection is a witty legislative-loophole idea that fits Heineken's pub-economy stake, with the audio-guide layer giving each pub the sonic furniture of a real museum. The native-to-category fit is partial: the audio guide is real audio craft, but the campaign's centre of gravity is the AR app and the legal mechanism, not voice or sound, so it sits at the edge of the Audio & Radio remit.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is the global Dutch brewery whose business model in Ireland depends on the on-trade pub channel, so protecting Irish pubs from closure is direct commercial self-interest dressed as cultural preservation — exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context cited is data-backed and verifiable ('a quarter of Irish pubs have shut since 2005', a widely-reported VFI/DIGI statistic), and the Irish Heritage Council's actual tax-relief framework for accredited heritage venues is a real legal hook the campaign exploits.

execution.rationale: The audio component is a museum-style narrated audio guide layered through an AR-triggered scan inside participating pubs, paired with 3D artefact descriptions and souvenir purchase prompts. The orchestration covers in-venue activation, the heritage-accreditation legal process, and earned media across Irish and international press, but the board provides little detail on the audio-craft choices themselves (voice talent, sound design, script tone), which keeps the audio execution undemonstrated for a Lions category that lives on craft.

impact.rationale: The '20% tax cut for participating pubs' is the right business outcome for the brand's on-trade real-estate problem and is anchored to a real statutory benefit, but the board does not disclose how many pubs achieved accreditation, how much aggregate tax saving was unlocked, or any beer-volume lift through the participating venues. Press coverage across Yahoo, Bloomberg, FT, The Telegraph, Irish Independent and Adweek is a credible directional signal of earned reach, but treated as entrant-reported pickup rather than audited impact.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the legal-loophole reframe of pubs as accredited museums is a smart commercial-purpose hybrid, but the audio craft is light and the business outcome rests on a single tax-rate figure without a participating-venue base.

one_liner: Blow across the bottle to hear what's left.
search_doc:
Blow across the bottle to hear what's left.
Mechanisms: pitch-as-measurement, audio-as-product-utility, packaging-pain-point-solution, everyday-physics-hijack
Idea: If you can't weigh or see how much is left, now you can listen. Sound Scales is a mobile audio tool that enables people to know how much Baileys they have left by simply blowing across the top of the bottle.
Insight: Our iconic bottle has a problem. Its dark thick glass made to protect the liquid from external light also prevents people from knowing how much is left. This is also a business problem as many of the bottles stored could be empty.

full_record.file_path: 2024/AUDIO&RADIO/SILVER/Sound Scales - Baileys (VML, London _ VML, New York).jpeg

extracted.tagline: A mobile tool that enables people to tell how much Baileys they have left.
extracted.context: Baileys is a Christmas favorite. Being Ireland's go-to drink for the holidays is also a problem; once December ends, over 660k bottles go to the cupboards and shelves for the rest of the year.
extracted.insight: Our iconic bottle has a problem. Its dark thick glass made to protect the liquid from external light also prevents people from knowing how much is left. This is also a business problem as many of the bottles stored could be empty.
extracted.idea: If you can't weigh or see how much is left, now you can listen. Sound Scales is a mobile audio tool that enables people to know how much Baileys they have left by simply blowing across the top of the bottle.
execution: Step 1: Open soundscales.baileys.com and choose your bottle size
Step 2: Blow across the top as the app listens to the pitch frequency
Step 3: Find out how much quantity is left
Step 4: Unlock Baileys recipes and offers to repeat purchase

inferred.one_liner: Blow across the bottle to hear what's left.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A web app that listens to the pitch produced when blowing across the Baileys bottle turns an audio trick into a literal volume gauge, lifting off-season sales by 23% and brand sentiment by 93%.

visual.style_description: Cinematic editorial layout: a woman in a striped shirt blows across a Baileys bottle on the right, while a green sound-wave ribbon weaves through the gold 'Sound Scales' title on the left; four numbered phone-mockup steps and a 3-metric strip sit at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Using the physical principle that air blown across a bottle produces a pitch that varies with the air-column length, the campaign turns an everyday acoustic phenomenon into a literal Baileys volume gauge inside the iconic opaque bottle. The idea is unambiguously audio-native: it cannot be expressed as a film or a poster, because the whole user experience hinges on the listener producing a sound and the app interpreting its frequency.

strategy.rationale: Baileys is Diageo's Irish-cream liqueur, historically a Christmas SKU whose entire commercial problem is off-season dormancy in consumer cupboards. The board names the brand-specific pain point with a credible operational figure — 'over 660k bottles go to cupboards once December ends' — and the dark-glass packaging that prevents visual fill-level checks is an observable product truth. Tying the audio utility to off-season repeat purchase is direct mission-fit, not borrowed cultural purpose.

execution.rationale: The web app uses pitch-frequency analysis on a recorded blow to estimate the remaining liquid volume based on the bottle's known geometry, with a four-step flow that ends in recipes and offers to drive repurchase. The craft is in the acoustic-physics calibration rather than in voice or composition, and the board does not detail sound-design choices (voice talent, audio UI cues, accuracy validation), so the audio execution is mechanically clever but lightly documented for the category.

impact.rationale: The '+23% direct sales' figure is the right business outcome for an off-season repurchase problem — sales, not impressions — and is described as 'highest off-season consumption', though the absolute baseline is not on the board. '99% user engagement' and '+93% positive brand sentiment' are unanchored ratios with no defined denominator ('engagement among whom?', 'sentiment vs what baseline?'), and press support is thin (Trendhunter, The Telegraph, Daily Mail), so the load-bearing claim is the sales lift.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because pitch-as-measurement is a genuinely audio-native product utility solving a real packaging pain point, with a sales lift to anchor it — held below Gold by unbased engagement percentages and light audio-craft documentation.

search_doc:
Dogs' tails become the conductor.
Mechanisms: animal-as-conductor, music-as-product-demo, data-translation-to-sound, purpose-driven-streaming, orchestra-as-stunt
Idea: We woke up with the renowned Radio Filharmonia Orkestra to create an audio-listening experience, taking dogs' wow-as-conductors of the orchestra. They sat in an symphonica based on dogs' personalities. Then, the dogs were rewarded with different sheets, and the musicians followed cues to set the love-meaning the songs. Every single dog wagging — patterns, study, and posture wave to ascribe their fluffy and excited expressions. Through music we ground low much dogs love Pedigree treats and how they can recreate the bonding relationship between dogs and their owners.
Insight: Studies have proven that the wagging tails reaction is a full range of emotion. What if we translated those into the language? To recreate the joy that Pedigree treats can bring to dogs and their humans?

extracted.tagline: An orchestra conducted by dogs' tails shows how much dogs love Pedigree treats.
extracted.context: Treating dogs is one of the purest ways every loving dog owner expresses their affection, but due to inflation and economic instability, dog parents worldwide have been cutting back on buying their dogs because they think treats are unnecessary or expensive.
extracted.insight: Studies have proven that the wagging tails reaction is a full range of emotion. What if we translated those into the language? To recreate the joy that Pedigree treats can bring to dogs and their humans?
extracted.idea: We woke up with the renowned Radio Filharmonia Orkestra to create an audio-listening experience, taking dogs' wow-as-conductors of the orchestra. They sat in an symphonica based on dogs' personalities. Then, the dogs were rewarded with different sheets, and the musicians followed cues to set the love-meaning the songs. Every single dog wagging — patterns, study, and posture wave to ascribe their fluffy and excited expressions. Through music we ground low much dogs love Pedigree treats and how they can recreate the bonding relationship between dogs and their owners.
execution: Live performance with Radio Filharmonia Orkestra conducted by dogs' tails
Original score composition translated from dogs' tail wagging
Streaming royalties from the music donated to dog shelters
Distributed across Spotify, Apple Music and other streaming platforms
metrics: +960% increase in searches for Pedigree treats
390,422 streaming royalties donated to dog shelters
press_coverage: The perfect combination: dogs and music — TIME
The soundtrack of a dog's joy — Axios

inferred.qualitative_summary: Translated dogs' tail-wag patterns into an original symphonic score performed by a live orchestra, driving a 960% lift in Pedigree-treat searches and 327M impressions, while routing streaming royalties to shelters as 'the most successful organic content in Pedigree history'.

visual.style_description: Cinematic golden-toned concert-hall hero shot of a single weimaraner standing in front of a seated orchestra, with the campaign title set in elegant serif italic above; right column carries the three big metric tiles, bottom strip shows five press-quote cards over photos of conducted dogs.

idea.rationale: Translating dogs' tail-wagging patterns into an orchestral score performed by a real philharmonic is a charming data-to-sound translation that demonstrates the joy treats provide rather than asserting it. The audio-native fit is medium-high — the output is a real piece of music distributed on streaming — but the demonstration of the dog-to-score translation depends heavily on the film case to be understood, limiting how purely the idea lives in audio.

strategy.rationale: Pedigree is Mars's global dog-food brand and the dominant dog-treats player whose 'Dogs Rule' / 'Feed the Good' platform anchors decades of pet-bonding work, so framing treats as an emotional-bonding necessity rather than a discretionary purchase is on-mission. The cited 'studies have proven wagging tails reaction is a full range of emotion' is presented without a source, and the broader claim that dog parents are cutting back on treats due to inflation is plausible but anchored on the board only as a setup line.

execution.rationale: The production pairs a live orchestra — described as the Radio Filharmonia Orkestra — with a composition derived from observed tail-wag patterns, with musicians cued to dog responses, distributed across Spotify, Apple Music and other streaming services and routing royalties to dog shelters. The board provides only sparse detail on the actual translation methodology (how a wag becomes a note, which conductor framework was used), so the audio-craft layer reads as a beautiful concept-piece rather than a fully documented sound-design system.

impact.rationale: '+960% increase in searches for Pedigree treats' is unanchored — 960% above what baseline window, in which markets? — and '327M impressions' is an entrant-reported reach figure. '390,422 streaming royalties donated to dog shelters' is concrete and the right kind of behaviour-change proxy for a purpose layer, though the currency unit is missing on the board. Press support (TIME, People, Axios, Yahoo, Last.fm) is a credible signal of earned conversation; the missing piece is treats sales lift, which would be the natural business outcome for a campaign whose stated context is consumers cutting back on treats.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the tail-conducted orchestra is a warm and shareable music-as-product-demo with credible press, but the search-lift percentage is unbased and the actual treats-sales outcome the strategy targets is absent from the board.

one_liner: 24 hours of radio where every song says 'Mercedes'.
search_doc:
24 hours of radio where every song says 'Mercedes'.
Mechanisms: playlist-as-brand-archive, radio-station-takeover, lyrics-as-media-asset, data-as-content
Idea: To bring this to the forefront, we partnered with Radio Nova — a musical radio institution in France — for a 24-hour station takeover. The Iconic Playlist. We took over the airwaves, playing tracks exclusively mentioning Mercedes-Benz and delving into the history of music with [...] showcasing the deep connection between artists and the brand.
Insight: Mercedes-Benz is the most name-checked car brand in music history — the brand's iconic status already lives inside popular music, it just needed to be played back.

extracted.tagline: A 24-hour radio takeover, featuring only tracks mentioning Mercedes-Benz.
extracted.context: What do thousands of music artists all have in common? Mercedes-Benz. From Janis Joplin and Beyoncé to Dua Lipa, Ariana Grande, Eagles, Eminem, Future, Skepta, Migos, Tom Petty and Lana Del Rey: Mercedes-Benz is the most mentioned car brand in music history. The figure continues to grow annually, defining the brand as a profound impact on artists and music genres, spanning from R&B to funk to pop rock, and highlighting its enduring legacy in pop culture.
extracted.idea: To bring this to the forefront, we partnered with Radio Nova — a musical radio institution in France — for a 24-hour station takeover. The Iconic Playlist. We took over the airwaves, playing tracks exclusively mentioning Mercedes-Benz and delving into the history of music with [...] showcasing the deep connection between artists and the brand.
execution: Partnership with Radio Nova for a 24-hour station takeover
Limited-edition collectable double vinyl release
Featuring 4 unique sleeve designs that showcase tracks from artists like Kendrick Lamar, Ella Fitzgerald, and Billie Eilish
Available via Universal Music
15 podcasts on radio and online
Pieces of music journalism representing different music styles

inferred.insight: Mercedes-Benz is the most name-checked car brand in music history — the brand's iconic status already lives inside popular music, it just needed to be played back.
inferred.one_liner: 24 hours of radio where every song says 'Mercedes'.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A day-long takeover of Radio Nova playing only songs that name-check Mercedes turned decades of accidental brand mentions into a curated music heritage moment, with 756 mentions in 24h and 44M organic reach.

visual.style_description: Editorial black layout dominated by a central close-up of the Mercedes-Benz star embossed on the front of a vinyl record sleeve labelled 'The Iconic Playlist'; press quotes column, metric block and OOH/digital thumbnail strip arranged around it; graffiti-script title and microphone imagery at the top.

idea.rationale: A 24-hour radio takeover playing only songs that name-check Mercedes-Benz reframes decades of accidental lyric mentions as a curated brand-archive piece, which is conceptually adjacent to the Budweiser 'Uninterruptads' idea but works through curation rather than ad-slot inversion. The audio-native fit is strong — the campaign only exists on air and on vinyl — though the mechanic itself is more a programmed exhibition than a fresh audio device.

strategy.rationale: Mercedes-Benz is the German luxury-vehicle marque whose decades-long cultural status is a documented business asset (hip-hop lyric counts, pop-culture iconography reports), and positioning the brand as 'the most mentioned car brand in music history' is a factual heritage claim supported by multiple published music-database analyses. The mission-fit is luxury-brand-prestige reinforcement rather than commercial push, which is appropriate for a French market premium-positioning play.

execution.rationale: The takeover ran on Radio Nova — a credible French musical-radio institution — across 24 hours with curated lyric-mentioning tracks (Kendrick Lamar, Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Eilish, Joplin, Beyoncé, Dua Lipa), backed by 15 podcasts and a limited-edition double-vinyl release with four sleeve designs via Universal Music. The orchestration is well staged but the actual audio craft is mostly curation and DJ programming rather than original sound design, voice or composition, which limits how much an Audio & Radio jury can credit it as audio craftsmanship.

impact.rationale: '756 Mercedes-Benz radio mentions in 24h' is a concrete, anchored count that matches the campaign's stated mechanism, and '+44M organic reach' is treated as entrant-reported and unaudited. The board does not show vehicle-consideration lift, configurator visits, dealer footfall or any business outcome a premium auto brand would normally track — the case rests on mentions-counted and a reach claim, with press support from Le Monde, Le Figaro, Forbes, GQ, Les Inrocks signalling real French-market conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the takeover is a coherent music-heritage piece that earned real French cultural press, but the mechanic is curation rather than craft and the impact case is mentions and reach rather than any auto-business outcome.

one_liner: The misheard lyric IS the hearing test.
search_doc:
The misheard lyric IS the hearing test.
Mechanisms: song-as-diagnostic-tool, cultural-artifact-hijack, earned-media-stunt, audio-as-product-demo
Idea: Re-record Rick Astley's 'Never Gonna Give You Up' with commonly misheard lyrics, releasing it without explanation to create the first mass hearing test.
Insight: Hearing loss is scary and isolating, but misheard entertains and connects.

extracted.tagline: The world's first mass hearing test.
extracted.context: People avoid hearing tests for up to 10 years because hearing loss terrifies them.
extracted.insight: Hearing loss is scary and isolating, but misheard entertains and connects.
extracted.idea: Re-record Rick Astley's 'Never Gonna Give You Up' with commonly misheard lyrics, releasing it without explanation to create the first mass hearing test.

inferred.one_liner: The misheard lyric IS the hearing test.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A re-released Rick Astley track with deliberately misheard lyrics turned a beloved pop song into a mass hearing test, smashing booking targets by 1220% and earning 95% UK news pickup.

visual.style_description: Two side-by-side hero portraits — the young 1980s Rick Astley next to present-day Rick Astley flanking a vintage microphone — frame the campaign title in tall serif caps; right-hand metric column in bold, press quotes and broadcast logos line the bottom.

idea.rationale: Re-recording a globally recognised pop song with its most commonly misheard lyrics turns the song itself into a self-administered hearing test, collapsing the gap between cultural artefact and diagnostic tool. The mechanic is native to audio: the joke and the diagnosis only exist when you hear the track, making it a purebred radio/streaming idea rather than a film-derived asset.

strategy.rationale: Specsavers is the UK and Ireland's largest optical and audiology retail chain, whose hearing-care business has long fought a cultural avoidance reflex around hearing loss; turning a fun cultural hijack into a free, frictionless self-test is squarely on brand mission. The stated insight — that people delay hearing tests for up to a decade because hearing loss is scary and isolating — is a documented audiology truth that the campaign reframes via the universal experience of misheard lyrics. The 'up to 10 years' figure is not sourced on the board, which is the one soft spot.

execution.rationale: The audio craft is precise: the Rick Astley re-record reproduces the original vocal performance with the misheard alternative lyrics swapped in line for line, so the joke only lands when the listener notices a mismatch between memory and what they hear. The release was staged without explanation as a coordinated radio, streaming and PR drop with the original artist on board, generating press loops across UK national broadcast and print that converted a cultural moment into hearing-test bookings.

impact.rationale: The headline '1220% above target' on hearing-test bookings is an anchored business outcome for an audiology retailer — bookings are the brand's actual KPI, not a surrogate metric — though the absolute booking volume and the original target size are not disclosed, so the multiplier is directional rather than fully verifiable. The '95% major news outlets' and '20M organic views' read as entrant-reported earned reach claims, but the on-board logo wall (BBC, Sky News, ITV News, The Times, Daily Mail, Evening Standard, Mirror, Huffpost, This Morning, NME) is a credible signal of national press penetration around a hearing-care subject that normally gets no media oxygen.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because it converts a beloved pop song into the world's first mass hearing test, making the audio medium itself the diagnostic instrument and translating that into a hard booking lift on Specsavers' own KPI.

one_liner: The insured car becomes the recording studio.
search_doc:
The insured car becomes the recording studio.
Mechanisms: product-as-platform, vehicle-as-studio, creator-economy-partnership, category-disruption
Idea: Turn an insured car into a mobile recording studio for TikTok artists, so the brand's core product (the car) becomes the launchpad for emerging music careers.
Insight: Aspiring TikTok musicians need studio time they can't afford — and many of them spend hours in their cars between gigs.

full_record.file_path: 2024/AUDIO&RADIO/SILVER/The Sound Studio - The General Insurance (ENERGY BBDO, Chicago).jpg.webp

extracted.tagline: Reimagining the recording studio to help TikTok artists make it big.
execution: A car converted into a fully-equipped mobile recording studio
TikTok creators record tracks inside the vehicle
Branded partnership content with TikTok artists
Video case content and behind-the-scenes documentation

inferred.context: The General Insurance has long relied on Shaquille O'Neal-led humour spots but wanted relevance with younger TikTok-native audiences who are unlikely to think about car insurance.
inferred.insight: Aspiring TikTok musicians need studio time they can't afford — and many of them spend hours in their cars between gigs.
inferred.idea: Turn an insured car into a mobile recording studio for TikTok artists, so the brand's core product (the car) becomes the launchpad for emerging music careers.
inferred.one_liner: The insured car becomes the recording studio.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repositions an insurance brand as a launchpad for TikTok music careers by turning the insured car itself into a recording studio; delivered 360M views, 935M impressions and a +1M follower lift while earning a 'first-of-its-kind' CBS write-up.

visual.style_description: Dark hero photograph of a black SUV with its hood and rear hatch open, glowing green from inside with studio equipment visible, and a giant 'SOUND STUDIO' wordmark in metallic chrome arched across the top; right column shows press pull-quotes, lower strip is a row of session photos of TikTok artists recording, and the bottom band lists results numbers (360M / 1M / 935M / DOUBLED / +32.5%) over partner and press logos.

idea.rationale: Turning the insured product (a car) into a mobile recording studio for TikTok creators is a clean product-as-platform move that lets a low-interest insurance brand earn relevance with an audience that ignores it. The audio-native fit is partial: the music output is audio, but the campaign's most cited assets are the visual case content and TikTok video views, so the idea works in audio rather than being born in it.

strategy.rationale: The General is a US non-standard auto-insurance carrier (Sentry/American Family-owned) historically positioned around Shaquille O'Neal humour spots for hard-to-insure drivers, which makes a Gen Z TikTok-music relevance play a deliberate audience expansion. The insight that aspiring TikTok musicians lack studio access while spending hours in their cars is plausible but unsourced on the board, and the linkage from 'rapper records in car' back to 'buys car insurance' is more associative than mechanistic.

execution.rationale: The car is rigged as a fully-equipped mobile recording studio with creator partnership content built around it, with original tracks released through TikTok artists and supporting documentary case content. The orchestration spans the partnership shoots, TikTok publishing, and an earned-press loop, though the board light on the audio-engineering choices that would make a Lions Audio jury credit the recording itself as craft (mic chain, vehicle acoustic treatment, mix style).

impact.rationale: '360 million views' and '935 million impressions' are headline reach figures treated as entrant-reported and unaudited, weakened further by two metrics labelled simply 'doubled' and '+32.5% increase' with no named base — the board does not say what doubled or what rose by 32.5%. '+1 million followers' is concrete but a surrogate brand metric; the actual business outcome for an insurer (policies quoted, sold, or retention lift) is conspicuously absent. Press coverage across Adweek, Forbes, The Drum, Complex, Mashable, Vice, CBS and Fox is a credible signal of earned conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because reframing the insured car as a creator studio is a smart category-disruption move with strong TikTok-scale reach, but two unlabelled percentages and the absence of an insurance business outcome cap how hard the impact case can land.

one_liner: Radio ads in the country's least-spoken language.
search_doc:
Radio ads in the country's least-spoken language.
Mechanisms: brand-name-as-universal-language, minority-language-targeting, radio-as-inclusion, linguistic-paradox
Idea: We created a global radio campaign in the least spoken language of each country with a message so that all those minorities who miss their language feel closer to home. Although the message was only understood by a few, everybody hears it as a Coca-Cola ad. After all, Coca-Cola is one of the most universally recognized words across the globe.
Insight: Even when listeners cannot understand the language of a radio spot, the word 'Coca-Cola' inside it is enough for everyone to recognise it as a Coca-Cola ad.

full_record.file_path: 2024/AUDIO&RADIO/BRONZE/The Universal Language of Coca-Cola - Coca-Cola (GUT, Buenos Aires).jpg

extracted.tagline: The most known word in the world after 'OK'.
extracted.idea: We created a global radio campaign in the least spoken language of each country with a message so that all those minorities who miss their language feel closer to home. Although the message was only understood by a few, everybody hears it as a Coca-Cola ad. After all, Coca-Cola is one of the most universally recognized words across the globe.
execution: Global radio campaign localised in the least-spoken language of each country
A message for 4,695 Polish in Turkey — where 79.3 million understand the word Coca-Cola
A message for 1,248 Koreans in Mexico — where 121.6 million understand the word Coca-Cola
A message for 53 Icelanders in Greece — where 9.7 million understand the word Coca-Cola
A message for 36 Maltese in Brazil — where 200 million understand the word Coca-Cola
A message for 3 Samoans in Norway — where 5 million understand the word Coca-Cola
metrics: 94% of the world's population knows the word Coca-Cola

inferred.context: Coca-Cola is one of the most universally recognised words on Earth, while migrant minorities can spend years without hearing their mother tongue on public airwaves.
inferred.insight: Even when listeners cannot understand the language of a radio spot, the word 'Coca-Cola' inside it is enough for everyone to recognise it as a Coca-Cola ad.
inferred.one_liner: Radio ads in the country's least-spoken language.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframes Coca-Cola's globally recognised brand name as a tool for linguistic inclusion, broadcasting radio spots in each country's smallest immigrant community language while still being understood as a Coca-Cola ad by everyone else.

visual.style_description: Light grey world map decorated with audio-waveform scribbles of the Coca-Cola logo overlaid on different continents; centre dominated by the giant red serif headline 'The UNIVERSAL LANGUAGE of Coca-Cola' with a script Coca-Cola signature woven through a sound waveform, supported by a five-column results row of stylised statistics ('A message for 4695 Polish in Turkey…') in Coca-Cola red.

idea.rationale: Running radio spots in each country's least-spoken immigrant language — knowing that majority listeners cannot understand the words but will still recognise the brand name embedded in them — is a sharp linguistic-paradox idea that uses Coca-Cola's universal-recognition status as the radio device itself. The audio-native fit is strong: the whole concept only works when an unintelligible language carries a recognisable phoneme inside it, which is purely an auditory effect.

strategy.rationale: Coca-Cola is the Coca-Cola Company's flagship cola brand whose long-running 'open happiness' / inclusion-themed brand platform makes minority-community outreach exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The strategic premise — that 'Coca-Cola' is the most-recognised word in the world after 'OK' — is a well-circulated branding factoid that, while widely cited, is presented on the board without a primary source. The minority-population figures named per market (4,695 Polish in Turkey, 1,248 Koreans in Mexico, 53 Icelanders in Greece, 36 Maltese in Brazil, 3 Samoans in Norway) are specific and verifiable-feeling.

execution.rationale: Each market got a radio spot scripted and recorded in its smallest immigrant community language, with the brand name preserved in its standard phonetic form so it punches through the unintelligibility. The orchestration is a globally-distributed radio rollout across multiple markets, but the board provides no audio-craft detail (voice direction, sound design, music bed) and no count of the actual languages produced or stations carrying them, leaving the production scale undocumented.

impact.rationale: The only stated metric is '94% of the world's population knows the word Coca-Cola', which is a brand-recognition factoid used to frame the strategy rather than a measured campaign outcome. The board carries no listener-recall figures, no minority-community response data, no media reach numbers and no press coverage at all, so the Impact case effectively rests on the elegance of the construct. For a global FMCG advocacy idea, the absence of any measured downstream consequence (inclusion sentiment, minority-community feedback, earned press) is the dominant Impact weakness.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the linguistic-paradox idea is a clever audio-native inclusion device, but the on-board impact rests on a single brand-recognition factoid with no measured campaign outcome or press support.

search_doc:
A motion picture without pictures.
Mechanisms: sound-as-cinematography, accessibility-as-craft, category-format-inversion, co-creation-with-community, product-launch-as-art-film
Idea: To launch Touch Card — a bank card designed for the blind and low vision community — Mastercard flipped the script to create an inclusive cinema experience that was truly priceless. TOUCH is a feature-length motion picture without pictures, brought to life entirely by Dolby sound. An original story about a neuroscientist trapped inside his father's mind.
Insight: Sighted-default cinema treats audio description as a bolt-on; for the blind and low-vision community, the film itself can be the description.

extracted.tagline: A motion picture without pictures. Using sound to make the cinema more inclusive.
extracted.context: Over 500 million people visit the cinema every year. Yet for those who are blind or have low vision, the experience is an afterthought. They rely on audio descriptions, screen readers or a companion to explain what's on the screen.
extracted.idea: To launch Touch Card — a bank card designed for the blind and low vision community — Mastercard flipped the script to create an inclusive cinema experience that was truly priceless. TOUCH is a feature-length motion picture without pictures, brought to life entirely by Dolby sound. An original story about a neuroscientist trapped inside his father's mind.
execution: Feature-length motion picture without pictures, made entirely with sound
Brought to life entirely by Dolby 7.1 sound
Every scene storyboarded to map sounds
An original score with 17 pieces of music
Classic film techniques told through sound
Refined with the blind and low vision community

By partnering non-sighted and sighted sound designers and engineers, we were able to innovate new ways of using sound in film. Spatial panning, subharmonics and original foley created immersive sonic environments that didn't need audio descriptions
TOUCH premiered at iconic motion theatres across Australia, where both sighted and non-sighted people were invited to experience the film together
metrics: After one premiere, the project garnered +AUD $10 million in earned media coverage and reached 74+ million people
300-500 more tracks than most blockbuster movies
press_coverage: SCREEN hub — Redefining what the cinema experience could be
The NewDaily — The motion picture without pictures, but with a purpose
Refinery29 — More than just accessible. It's revolutionary
Mamamia — The best film I've ever heard

inferred.insight: Sighted-default cinema treats audio description as a bolt-on; for the blind and low-vision community, the film itself can be the description.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A feature-length film made entirely through Dolby 7.1 sound to launch the Touch Card; co-created with blind and low-vision sound designers, premiered theatrically, generated AUD $10M+ in earned media and reached 74M+ people.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dark navy board with the title 'TOUCH' rendered as a soft motion-blur cinema lightbox; six small black-and-white production stills (storyboards, foley, studio sessions) sit in the middle column with explanatory captions, dense left-hand body copy, and a column of press pull-quote cards down the right edge with subtle waveform graphics top and bottom.

idea.rationale: A feature-length 'motion picture without pictures' made entirely from Dolby 7.1 sound to launch the Touch Card for blind and low-vision customers is a clean category-format inversion that turns accessibility from a bolt-on into the medium itself. The audio-native fit is total — the whole artefact only exists in sound — and the conceit is born from the product launch rather than retrofitted, anchoring an unusually ambitious audio scope for the category.

strategy.rationale: Mastercard is the global payments network whose 'Priceless' platform has consistently funded experience-led brand work, and the Touch Card is its accessibility-designed bank card for blind and low-vision users — so a fully accessible cinema launch is exact product-mission fit rather than borrowed purpose. The cited context — 500 million annual cinema visitors with blind and low-vision audiences treated as an afterthought — is plausible-feeling but the source for the 500M figure is not on the board, and the load-bearing accessibility insight comes via genuine co-creation with the blind and low-vision community rather than a single sourced statistic.

execution.rationale: The film was scripted, storyboarded scene-by-scene to map sounds, then built with spatial panning, subharmonics and original foley plus a 17-piece original score, co-designed by paired sighted and non-sighted sound designers and engineers — the board claims '300-500 more tracks than most blockbuster movies'. It premiered theatrically in Dolby 7.1 with mixed sighted and non-sighted audiences, which gives the audio craft a serious cinema-spec showcase and is the strongest evidence on the board.

impact.rationale: 'AUD $10M+ in earned media' and '74M+ people reached' are entrant-reported reach figures from a single premiere, with the press wall (Refinery29, Mamamia, news.com.au, The NewDaily, Screen Hub, 10 News) carrying expressive superlative quotes ('gobsmacking', 'groundbreaking', 'revolutionary') that should be treated as critic opinion rather than measured outcome. The actual Touch Card business outcome — card issuance, activations, accessibility customer uptake — is not on the board, so for a product launch the most direct commercial proof point is absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the picture-less Dolby feature is unusually ambitious audio craft on exact product mission, even though the impact case rests on a single premiere's reach claim and critic praise rather than Touch Card uptake.

one_liner: The song lyric becomes the Budweiser ad.
search_doc:
The song lyric becomes the Budweiser ad.
Mechanisms: lyric-as-media, platform-jailbreak, interruption-as-non-interruption, playlist-hijack
Idea: We turned songs with Budweiser in the lyrics into Spotify ads, blending into people's playlists to not interrupt your vibes. Are you enjoying a rap playlist? It was a Beastie Boys song that captures the ad and doesn't interrupt your vibe. Enjoying a funky playlist? Pop? Rock? Country? Soul? Well, you got the idea.
Insight: Spotify free listeners hate being interrupted by ads — but they happily listen to songs that already mention the brand.

extracted.tagline: Songs with Budweiser in the lyrics became Spotify ads to not interrupt your vibe.
extracted.context: Music is part of Budweiser's history, and Budweiser is literally part of music history. There are over 500 songs making unofficial advertising for the brand, but no one ever made them official, right where people hate to be interrupted: Spotify free account.
extracted.idea: We turned songs with Budweiser in the lyrics into Spotify ads, blending into people's playlists to not interrupt your vibes. Are you enjoying a rap playlist? It was a Beastie Boys song that captures the ad and doesn't interrupt your vibe. Enjoying a funky playlist? Pop? Rock? Country? Soul? Well, you got the idea.
execution: Spotify free account audio ads
Songs naturally containing the word 'Budweiser' served as the ad spot
Rap, Pop, Rock, Country, Soul playlists targeted by genre
Curated playlist of Budweiser-mentioning tracks (Beastie Boys 'Posse in Effect', Kesha, John Conlee, Frank Zappa, R-Kid, Bullhead, etc.)
metrics: 8x more earned media than paid media
press_coverage: AdAge — 'Spotify free users, Budweiser hears you'

inferred.insight: Spotify free listeners hate being interrupted by ads — but they happily listen to songs that already mention the brand.
inferred.one_liner: The song lyric becomes the Budweiser ad.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns pre-existing lyric mentions of Budweiser into native Spotify ad slots so the brand reaches free-tier listeners without interrupting them; earned 8x its paid spend in media coverage with zero listener backlash.

visual.style_description: Clean white case-study layout centred on a large iPhone mockup playing a 'Beastie Boys – Posse in Effect' Spotify ad card with a Budweiser headline; left column carries the Music/Idea/Execution copy in red, right column displays a grid of album covers labelled by genre (Rap, Pop, R&B, J-Pop, Rock, Vocal Group) with the bold 'UNINTERRUPTADS' wordmark across the top.

idea.rationale: Turning pre-existing song lyrics that already say 'Budweiser' into Spotify free-tier audio ads is a clean platform-native inversion: the ad break becomes the music, so the brand reaches an audience that hates being interrupted by not interrupting them. The conceit lives purely in audio — it cannot be a film, a poster or a stunt — which is exactly what a Lion in this category rewards.

strategy.rationale: Budweiser is Anheuser-Busch InBev's flagship American lager whose long-running brand platform anchors music sponsorship (Made in America, Bud Stage, decades of artist partnerships), so reclaiming the 500+ songs that already namecheck the brand is exact heritage-fit rather than borrowed culture. The insight that free-tier Spotify listeners resent interruption is a well-established platform pain point, and the board cites '500+ songs' as the addressable inventory but does not source that count, which is the soft spot.

execution.rationale: The mechanic is craft-precise: each ad slot is a real-track excerpt where the brand name occurs naturally in the lyric (Beastie Boys, Kesha, Frank Zappa, John Conlee), served by genre into the free-tier audio break so the targeting matches the listener's playlist context (rap into rap, country into country). The orchestration extends from Spotify ad delivery into a curated public playlist, with the platform itself doing the amplification once listeners realised the ad and the music were the same artefact.

impact.rationale: '8x more earned media than paid media' is an anchored ratio that names the multiplier and the denominator, and 'people angry with the ads: 0' is a witty but verifiable zero-complaint claim on a notoriously irritation-prone format. The '+40K people impacted per hour' figure is unanchored — 40K of what universe, and what counts as 'impacted'? — and the on-board press support is thin (Billboard, AdAge, Contagious quotes only), so the business outcome a beer brand cares about most (volume or share lift) is not present on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the lyric IS the ad — a platform-native inversion that turns 500+ accidental brand mentions into intentional Spotify media without interrupting the listener, validated by an 8:1 earned-to-paid ratio.

one_liner: Radio shows what print refused to show.
search_doc:
Radio shows what print refused to show.
Mechanisms: medium-as-message, absence-of-image-as-craft, print-to-audio-translation, advocacy-through-restraint
Idea: Adapt the 'Unshowable' print concept to radio — using audio to convey the realities of vulnerable children that cannot be shown visually, so that a donation can help to change the way they see the world.
Insight: Just like in print, a very simple idea: radio is the perfect medium to convey what millions of children live every day. Because on radio, there's nothing to see. And when it comes to the wedding night of an 11 year old girl or the cries of a sexually mutilated baby, it is better not to see.

extracted.tagline: The good thing about radio is that there's nothing to see.
extracted.context: World Vision was created in 1947 as an NGO in order to help vulnerable children all over the world. In 2022, they had run the 'Unshowable' print campaign. In 2023, they wanted to adapt the concept to a totally different media: radio.
extracted.insight: Just like in print, a very simple idea: radio is the perfect medium to convey what millions of children live every day. Because on radio, there's nothing to see. And when it comes to the wedding night of an 11 year old girl or the cries of a sexually mutilated baby, it is better not to see.
extracted.idea: Adapt the 'Unshowable' print concept to radio — using audio to convey the realities of vulnerable children that cannot be shown visually, so that a donation can help to change the way they see the world.
execution: Three radio spots adapted from the original print pieces
Each spot pairs with an 'Unshowable' print key visual showing the back of a child's head
metrics: +3% donations despite the highest inflation in France since 4 decades

inferred.one_liner: Radio shows what print refused to show.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Extending the 'Unshowable' print idea to radio uses the medium's invisibility to convey atrocities against children, lifting donations by 3% in a record-inflation year and pulling +30% traffic to the cause site.

visual.style_description: Three vertically stacked print key visuals — each a back-of-the-head portrait of a child against a war-zone, domestic-violence or famine backdrop — sit on the left of a dark layout; right side carries the campaign quote in large serif, supporting copy and a clean 3-line results block.

review_reasons: Board presents the radio extension but shows only the print key visuals; no waveform, script excerpt or audio credits are visible on the case board.

idea.rationale: Extending the 'Unshowable' print idea to radio uses the medium's invisibility as the very point — child-protection atrocities that cannot ethically be shown can be conveyed in audio, where there is nothing to see. The mechanic is purely audio-native and a clean medium-as-message device, though it is also a media-extension of an existing 2022 print campaign rather than an idea born for audio.

strategy.rationale: World Vision France is the French branch of the global Christian humanitarian NGO World Vision, founded in 1947 and focused on vulnerable-children advocacy — so confronting donors with the realities of child marriage, mutilation and sexual violence is direct mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context cited (1947 founding, prior 2022 print campaign) is verifiable, and the strategic argument that radio's blindness is uniquely suited to subjects 'better not seen' is internally coherent.

execution.rationale: Three radio spots were produced as audio translations of the original print key visuals (back-of-the-head portraits paired with war-zone, domestic-violence and famine backdrops), distributed presumably on French radio though the board does not specify stations, rotation, or duration. The board carries no script excerpt, no waveform, no audio credits and no sound-design detail — flagged at extraction for that reason — so the audio craft itself, which is what an Audio & Radio jury rewards, is undemonstrated on the case board.

impact.rationale: '+3% donations despite the highest inflation in France since 4 decades' is the right business outcome for an NGO and meaningfully contextualised by the inflationary environment, though the absolute donation base is not disclosed. '+30% traffic to the website' and '+15% engagement' are unanchored ratios with no defined baseline window, and the board carries no press coverage to externally validate the campaign's cultural reach. The inflation-context framing is genuinely helpful for interpreting the donation lift.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the medium-as-message extension is genuinely audio-native for a subject that cannot be shown, anchored by a real donation lift in a record-inflation year, but the case board provides no audio craft evidence and the supporting metrics lack absolute bases.
